title: Pedestrian Behavior in Shared Spaces with Autonomous Vehicles: Behavioral Modeling and Road Safety Implications

sections.name: Introduction
sections.summary: Contextualizes the AV era's disruptive challenge to pedestrian road-crossing heuristics built over decades of human-driven traffic. Notes that existing pedestrian behavioral models assume human-driver responses and that AVs break these assumptions through novel yielding patterns and communication modalities. Establishes review scope: AV-pedestrian interaction at crosswalks and shared spaces, 2015-2021.
sections.organization_pattern: AV deployment context -> pedestrian heuristic assumptions violated -> novel interaction types -> scope -> contribution
sections.citation_pattern: Foundational AV papers cited; early AV-pedestrian studies introduced to frame gap
useful_phrases: Pedestrian crossing behavior has long been modeled under the assumption that drivers are human: attentive, responsive, and capable of non-verbal negotiation.
This assumption is now being violated at scale as autonomous vehicles enter shared road spaces.
The behavioral adaptations required of pedestrians in AV environments represent a fundamentally different challenge from...
A central question for the field is whether pedestrians can calibrate their crossing heuristics to AV behavior patterns, and if so, over what timeframe.

sections.name: AV-Pedestrian Interaction Modalities
sections.summary: Organizes interaction modalities into three categories: (1) implicit interaction via vehicle motion cues (speed, acceleration profile, yielding trajectory), (2) explicit communication via external human-machine interfaces (eHMI) such as light bars, displays, and audio signals, (3) infrastructure-mediated interaction via V2P communication and smart crosswalks. Reviews behavioral evidence for each.
sections.organization_pattern: define modality -> review behavioral evidence -> compare modalities -> open implementation questions
sections.citation_pattern: experimental studies with controlled AV/vehicle behavior; notes sample limitations
useful_phrases: Implicit modality relies on the pedestrian's ability to read vehicle motion cues, a skill that transfers imperfectly from human-driven contexts because AVs...
eHMI designs range from... to..., but evidence for their effectiveness in improving pedestrian crossing decisions remains mixed.
The key unresolved question for eHMI design is whether...
Infrastructure-mediated approaches represent the most recently explored modality and offer the advantage of..., but depend on...

sections.name: Yielding Behavior Models
sections.summary: Reviews models applied to AV-pedestrian yielding: gaze-based collision avoidance models extended to AV contexts, social force models with modified vehicle repulsion terms, data-driven neural network models trained on AV interaction datasets. Notes that most models have been validated in simulated or controlled physical environments, with limited real-world transfer validation.
rhetorical_moves: extension/modification from prior human-driver models noted
sections.organization_pattern: model family -> origin/modification -> inputs -> validation evidence -> transfer limitations
sections.citation_pattern: model papers cited; transfer validation failures noted explicitly
useful_phrases: Social force models have been extended to AV-pedestrian interaction by introducing a modified repulsion term that accounts for...
While these models reproduce aggregate yielding patterns in simulation, their transfer to real-world AV deployment contexts remains largely untested.
The neural network approaches achieve higher AUC on benchmark AV-pedestrian datasets, but this performance gain comes at the cost of...
A critical gap in the validation literature is the absence of studies comparing model predictions against longitudinal field data from...

sections.name: Crossing Intent Prediction
sections.summary: Covers pedestrian crossing intent prediction models in AV context: video-based CNN/RNN approaches using body pose and gaze tracking, V2P communication-based approaches using smartphone data, multimodal fusion approaches combining vehicle sensors with infrastructure data. Performance metrics reviewed include AUC, precision-recall, and time-to-crossing accuracy.
rhetorical_moves: task definition (why intent prediction matters for AV safety)
sections.organization_pattern: task importance -> approach-1 with metrics -> approach-2 with metrics -> comparison table -> dataset limitation
sections.citation_pattern: benchmark papers cited; performance claims qualified by dataset specificity
useful_phrases: Accurate crossing intent prediction is a prerequisite for AV planning systems to achieve...
Video-based approaches achieve AUC values of 0.85-0.93 on benchmark datasets, but these benchmarks are predominantly from...
A recurring limitation across approaches is the short time horizon of predictions: most models are validated at 1-2 seconds pre-crossing, yet...
The absence of a unified benchmark dataset spanning diverse infrastructure contexts makes cross-study comparison problematic.

sections.name: Research Gaps
sections.summary: Five gaps: (1) longitudinal behavioral adaptation — do pedestrians update heuristics with sustained AV exposure? (2) cross-cultural variation in AV-pedestrian norms — most studies in North America/Europe (3) vulnerable populations (elderly, children, visually impaired) undersampled (4) eHMI standardization absent — designs vary wildly without performance standards (5) real-world transfer of lab/simulator models undocumented.
rhetorical_moves: each gap named precisely with structural cause
severity tied to AV deployment scale
sections.organization_pattern: gap statement -> partial evidence -> insufficiency -> why it matters at scale
sections.citation_pattern: each gap anchored to specific study or dataset limitation
useful_phrases: Gap 1 concerns behavioral adaptation: whether pedestrians recalibrate their gap-acceptance thresholds when interacting with consistently yielding AVs remains unknown because...
The cross-cultural gap is severe: over 80% of published AV-pedestrian studies were conducted in North American or European contexts, yet AV deployment is proceeding rapidly in...
Vulnerable population undersampling is particularly consequential because these groups show qualitatively different crossing heuristics that...
The simulator-to-real-world transfer gap represents a foundational validity concern: models validated exclusively in simulator may...

sections.name: Conclusion
sections.summary: Field-level synthesis: AV-pedestrian interaction modeling is nascent, methodologically diverse, and currently limited by short-horizon datasets and narrow cultural scope. Stable finding: implicit motion cues are read by pedestrians but incompletely; yielding reliability is the key calibrating signal. Unresolved: long-term behavioral adaptation. Priority: longitudinal field studies in diverse cultural contexts with standardized eHMI.
sections.organization_pattern: field framing -> stable conclusions -> open questions -> prioritized action
sections.citation_pattern: sparingly; key claims without per-paper citation

global_patterns.opening_strategy: paradigm shift framing: existing model assumptions violated by AV deployment
global_patterns.synthesis_strategy: per-modality/approach evidence then cross-method comparison with metric heterogeneity note
global_patterns.comparison_strategy: approach-by-approach on metrics + validation context; transfer limitations explicit
global_patterns.gap_strategy: named precisely with structural cause + deployment-scale consequence
global_patterns.ending_strategy: field framing -> stable -> unresolved -> prioritized longitudinal action

notes: Synthetic composite based on IEEE TITS / TRC AV-pedestrian review patterns 2019-2022. Used as both prose and structure reference for AV-related review sections.
